The first is : the data around the size of Government is absolutely crucial to this debate. So, when Deputy Kazantseva-Miller rose to tell us what S&P think, the first thing that went through my mind is, well, why didn’t S&P tell us at the same time that we had broken our own fiscal rules? Because the fiscal rules of this Assembly are that the amount that we take from our economy, from our GDP in tax, must not exceed 24%. And interestingly, if we chose to introduce GST, it still would not exceed 24%. This is absolutely crucial because these comparatives matter.

If you take the amount of tax we take as a percentage of our economy and if you take the amount of tax that Jersey takes as a percentage of its economy, the same for the Isle of Man, the numbers are 22% for us, 26% for Jersey and 29% for the Isle of Man. Those are simple arithmetic calculations. We could stand here today and argue until the cows come home whether our GDP is accurate or not. But on a reported and fiscally independently verified basis, those are the numbers.
